title: SEGMENT 10 — Translation, the frameshift, and trafficking to the membrane

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Translation, the frameshift, and trafficking to the membrane
Welcome back.
We are now in the cytoplasm of an HIV-infected cell, with several flavors of viral mRNA available.
Let's translate them.
The unspliced 9-kilobase transcript is loaded onto ribosomes.
By default, ribosomes translate it from the first AUG and produce Gag, a 55 kilodalton polyprotein, Pr55.
Gag contains, in order from N-terminus to C-terminus: MA, CA, NC, p6 — and a small spacer peptide called p2 between CA and NC, and another called p1 between NC and p6.
So Gag is a single polyprotein that contains all the structural components of the future virion.
But Gag is not the only thing that gets made from this transcript.
Gag is followed in the genome by Pol — protease, RT, integrase.
And the virus needs those enzymes inside every new particle.
The trick the virus uses to express Pol is one of the most elegant tricks in all of molecular biology: a −1 ribosomal frameshift.

Roughly 5% of ribosomes that translate Gag, on reaching a specific sequence near the C-terminus of Gag — a "slippery sequence" of the form X XXY YYZ — slip backwards by one nucleotide and continue translating in the −1 reading frame.
Downstream of the slippery sequence is a stable RNA pseudoknot that physically blocks the ribosome and increases the probability of slippage.
After the slip, the ribosome is now reading in a frame that fuses Gag to the entire Pol open reading frame, producing a Gag-Pol fusion polyprotein, Pr160.
The result is a defined stoichiometry: about 20 Gag polyproteins are made for every 1 Gag-Pol fusion.
That ratio matters enormously.
Gag is the structural building block; you need a lot of it.
The enzymes need to be present but in only a handful of copies per particle.
The frameshift sets the ratio precisely.
Modify the slippery sequence or the pseudoknot, perturb the ratio, and particle assembly falls apart.
Env is translated separately, from a singly spliced mRNA.
It enters the secretory pathway, gets heavily glycosylated in the endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi, and is cleaved by furin into SU and TM.
SU and TM remain non-covalently associated as a trimer of heterodimers and traffic to the plasma membrane, where they wait to be incorporated into budding particles.
The Gag polyprotein, meanwhile, has a critical modification on its N-terminus: a myristoyl group, a 14-carbon saturated fatty acid added by host N-myristoyltransferase.
The myristoyl group anchors Gag to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.
Gag also has a basic patch that interacts with phosphatidylinositol-(4,5)-bisphosphate, PIP2, a lipid that is enriched in the plasma membrane.
So Gag specifically targets the plasma membrane, and starts to oligomerize there.
There's important work in the postgrad literature on where exactly Gag assembles.
In T cells, Gag forms particles directly at the plasma membrane.
In macrophages, particles often appear to assemble in intracellular compartments that are connected to the cell surface by narrow channels.
The interpretation has gone back and forth.
The current view is that even in macrophages, those "intracellular" compartments are continuous with the surface and represent specialized membrane microdomains.
Gag also has to find a copy of the genomic RNA.
This is the role of the NC domain of Gag.
NC has two zinc finger motifs that recognize the psi packaging signal in the unspliced genomic RNA.
NC binds psi, and through this interaction Gag selects unspliced RNA for packaging — distinguishing it from cellular mRNAs and from spliced viral RNAs that have lost psi.
Two copies of genomic RNA are packaged per particle.
The two RNAs are linked at their 5' ends through a structured region called the dimerization initiation site.
So one Gag molecule, with one NC, grabs the RNA dimer; many more Gag molecules pile on through Gag-Gag interactions; the assembling structure curves outward and begins to bud.
That brings us to assembly and budding.
